fragments.content: Thyroid cancer is the most common endocrine malignancy. Four main histologic types: (1) Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) - 80-85% of cases, arises from follicular cells, often spreads via lymphatics, excellent prognosis; (2) Follicular thyroid carcinoma (FTC) - 10-15%, hematogenous spread to bone/lung, prognosis intermediate; (3) Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) - 3-5%, from parafollicular C-cells, secretes calcitonin, 25% hereditary (MEN2 syndromes); (4) Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) - 1-2%, undifferentiated, aggressive with poor prognosis (median survival months). Risk factors: radiation exposure (especially childhood), family history, iodine deficiency (follicular), genetic mutations (BRAF V600E in PTC, RET in MTC). Incidence increasing due to detection of small papillary cancers.

Primary treatment: surgical resection. Lobectomy sufficient for low-risk papillary cancers <4cm without extrathyroidal extension; total thyroidectomy for larger tumors, bilateral disease, high-risk features, or when radioactive iodine (RAI) planned. Central neck dissection for clinically involved nodes. Postoperative RAI ablation (I-131) for intermediate/high-risk differentiated thyroid cancer to destroy residual tissue and treat metastases. TSH suppression therapy with levothyroxine reduces recurrence risk in differentiated thyroid cancer (target TSH <0.1 mIU/L for high-risk, 0.1-0.5 for intermediate). External beam radiation for unresectable local disease. Targeted therapies for RAI-refractory disease: lenvatinib, sorafenib (differentiated); vandetanib, cabozantinib (medullary); dabrafenib/trametinib for BRAF-mutated anaplastic. Active surveillance appropriate for very low-risk microcarcinomas (<1cm).

Initial evaluation: high-resolution thyroid ultrasound characterizes nodules using TI-RADS or ATA patterns (hypoechogenicity, irregular margins, microcalcifications, taller-than-wide shape suggest malignancy). Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) biopsy indicated for nodules ≥1cm with suspicious features, or ≥1.5-2cm with intermediate suspicion. Bethesda System classifies FNA results: I (nondiagnostic), II (benign), III (atypia/follicular lesion of undetermined significance), IV (follicular neoplasm), V (suspicious for malignancy), VI (malignant). Molecular testing (Afirma, ThyroSeq) aids indeterminate nodules. Serum thyroglobulin for post-treatment surveillance of differentiated thyroid cancer. Calcitonin and CEA for medullary thyroid cancer diagnosis/monitoring. RET mutation testing for all MTC patients. Staging uses TNM system with age cutoff at 55 years for differentiated thyroid cancer.
